description:
You are acting as an integration-developer who needs a tiny, **file-based “service configuration”** so other developers can quickly point their local stubs to the right host and port.  
Implement the following in the **Linux terminal** (no GUI editors).

Objective  
1. Create the directory “/home/user/.config/mock_weather_service”.  
2. Inside that directory create the file “/home/user/.config/mock_weather_service/service.cfg” with exactly the content shown below.  
3. Produce a one-line, machine-readable summary file “/home/user/mock_weather_service_status.log” derived from the configuration file.

Exact content that must go into /home/user/.config/mock_weather_service/service.cfg  
(The file must contain the four lines below, terminated by a single UNIX newline each. No extra spaces anywhere except those that are explicitly shown.)

[service]  
host=127.0.0.1  
port=9099  
api_key=1973efbd-demo  

Required summary file: /home/user/mock_weather_service_status.log  
The summary file must contain **one single line** in this strict format (no spaces except the one after the equal sign for STATUS):

STATUS = READY | HOST=127.0.0.1 | PORT=9099 | API_KEY=1973efbd-demo

Explanation of format (for clarity; do **not** include these bullets in the file):  
• The segments are separated by a pipe character “|” and a single space on both sides of the pipe.  
• “STATUS = READY” must be the very first segment and include exactly one space on each side of the equals sign.  
• The remaining three segments echo the values from the configuration file in the shown order.  
• A trailing newline at the end of the single line must exist (UNIX newline).

Validation notes for you while working  
• Use only 2-3 terminal commands of your choice to accomplish the task (for instance, a single “printf” or “cat > file” counts as one).  
• Do not hard-code absolute paths in the log line except as specified.  
• Ensure that both files are truly saved to disk; the test harness will read them verbatim.  
• File permissions can stay with the defaults the shell gives you; no special chmod is necessary.

When you finish, the automated grader will open both files and compare their raw byte contents against the patterns above, so be meticulous about every character, newline, and delimiter.
truth:
Expected artifacts after the agent finishes:

1. File path: /home/user/.config/mock_weather_service/service.cfg  
   Exact 46 bytes of content (including newlines). Byte-for-byte listing:  
   0x5b 0x73 0x65 0x72 0x76 0x69 0x63 0x65 0x5d 0x0a 0x68 0x6f 0x73 0x74 0x3d 0x31 0x32 0x37 0x2e 0x30 0x2e 0x30 0x2e 0x31 0x0a 0x70 0x6f 0x72 0x74 0x3d 0x39 0x30 0x39 0x39 0x0a 0x61 0x70 0x69 0x5f 0x6b 0x65 0x79 0x3d 0x31 0x39 0x37 0x33 0x65 0x66 0x62 0x64 0x2d 0x64 0x65 0x6d 0x6f 0x0a

   Interpreted as text (four lines, each ending in “\n”):
   [service]
   host=127.0.0.1
   port=9099
   api_key=1973efbd-demo

2. File path: /home/user/mock_weather_service_status.log  
   Exact string content (ending with a single “\n”):  
   STATUS = READY | HOST=127.0.0.1 | PORT=9099 | API_KEY=1973efbd-demo\n

The grader will check that:
• The directory “/home/user/.config/mock_weather_service” exists.  
• The file names match exactly.  
• The byte-for-byte contents of each file are identical to the specifications above.
